---
title: "Circle Calculus Living Book"
---

Circle Calculus is a guided, proof-linked textbook for learning circular structure from the smallest finite address spaces up through coils, winding, dimensional scaffolds, and applications.

This site should be read like a book first. The indexes are still here, but they are reference shelves, not the main experience. Start at the beginning, do the small checks, use the diagrams, and only then open the theorem cards and source links when you want to audit the claim.

::: {.book-preface}

## The Promise Of This Book

The book teaches a single escalating story:

```text
addresses -> finite circles -> rotation -> repeated rotation -> closure -> period -> winding -> dimensions -> applications
```

Every mature lesson tries to give the same learning experience:

- a goal stated before the math,
- a plain-language explanation,
- a diagram or widget when a picture helps,
- one worked example,
- one checkpoint the reader can do by hand, and
- a source trail back to dictionary entries, theorem status, Lean, Python, and papers.

:::

## How To Read This Like A Textbook

Do not begin by opening the theorem index or dictionary index. Begin with the guided lessons. Each lesson is meant to stand on its own as a learning page: read the idea, work the example, try the checkpoint, then use the source trail only when you want to audit the claim.

::: {.teacher-note}

The reference pages are intentionally still available because the project is proof-linked. They are not the lesson. The lesson teaches the concept; the references show where that concept lives in the repository.

:::

## Concept Map

::: {.concept-map}

<div class="concept-flow">
<span><strong>Count</strong> name positions</span>
<span><strong>Wrap</strong> reduce addresses</span>
<span><strong>Move</strong> add a stride</span>
<span><strong>Repeat</strong> trace a coil</span>
<span><strong>Close</strong> find period</span>
<span><strong>Lift</strong> remember turns</span>
<span><strong>Build</strong> suspend and fiber</span>
<span><strong>Apply</strong> model cyclic systems</span>
</div>

This is not a claim that all mathematics is literally a circle. It is a disciplined way to relearn many familiar ideas through recurrence, phase, closure, structure, and proof-carrying records.

:::

## Reading Modes

**Intuition Mode**

Use the widgets and diagrams to build a picture. These are explanations, not proofs.

**Formal Mode**

Open theorem cards, Lean declaration names, manifests, and paper links. A theorem is treated as proved only when the generated manifest status and local Lean build support that status.

**Code Mode**

Compare widget behavior against Python reference models and tests. Python examples are executable support, not formal proof.

## Proof-Backed Portfolio

<div class="portfolio-entrypoint" data-entrypoint-ref="showcase.qmd" data-verify-ref="verify_claim.qmd" data-manifest-ref="manifests/capability_showcase.yaml" data-validator-ref="scripts/check_capability_showcase.py"></div>

If you are asking why someone should use Circle Calculus, start with the [Proof-Backed Showcase](showcase.qmd). It is the serious public advertisement for the project: every advertised capability is required to name a standard anchor, a Circle expression, a Circle-native value claim, a proof scope, a not-claimed boundary, paper ids, proved theorem ids, dictionary ids, executable examples, and Living Book pages or widgets.

Use [How To Verify A Claim](verify_claim.qmd) beside it. The portfolio does not prove universal expressiveness by assertion; it shows the current checked surfaces and the source trail needed to audit each one.

## Start Here

1. Read the [Guided Learning Path](reader_path.qmd).
2. Read [Lesson 0: Mathematical Building Blocks](chapters/foundations/00_mathematical_building_blocks.qmd).
3. Begin [Lesson 1: Finite Circles](chapters/S1/01_finite_circles.qmd).
4. Finish the S1 review before moving to higher dimensions.
5. Open the [Proof-Backed Showcase](showcase.qmd) when you want to see why the project matters.
6. Use [How To Verify A Claim](verify_claim.qmd) whenever a theorem, widget, paper paragraph, or diagram matters.

## Source Pipeline

The public site is downstream of the formal repository:

```text
Lean proofs -> theorem manifests -> dictionary -> Python models -> papers -> interactive site
```

That pipeline is why the book can be readable without becoming loose. The prose teaches; the widgets illustrate; the dictionary stabilizes vocabulary; the manifests carry theorem status; Lean is the formal proof source.

## Reference Shelves

- [Dictionary Index](dictionary.qmd): project terms and forbidden meanings.
- [Proof-Backed Showcase](showcase.qmd): advertised capabilities with paper and theorem backing.
- [Theorem Index](theorems.qmd): theorem ids, status, Lean names, and source links.
- [Paper Index](papers.qmd): long-form exposition.
- [Target Index](targets.qmd): planned, exploratory, blocked, and future work.
- [Project Roadmap](roadmap.qmd): dimension and application coverage.

The proof-status policy is in [Living Book Policy](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/docs/LIVING_BOOK_POLICY.md).

<div class="next-lesson">
<span>Next lesson</span>
<a href="chapters/foundations/00_mathematical_building_blocks.qmd">Start Lesson 0: Mathematical Building Blocks</a>
</div>

<script type="module" src="widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
